events.text: Patient is a 28-year-old Caucasian female who presented with menorrhagia, gingival hyperplasia, and bilateral lower extremity ecchymoses.
At age 28, the patient was diagnosed with acute myeloid leukemia with maturation, ICD-O morphology code 9874/3, primary site bone marrow C42.1.

At age 28, initial laboratory workup demonstrated white blood cell count of 86,000/uL with 61% blasts, hemoglobin 9.4 g/dL, and platelet count of 28,000/uL. LDH was 1,540 U/L and uric acid was 9.8 mg/dL. Coagulation was notable for fibrinogen 118 mg/dL and D-dimer 8.4 ug/mL.

At age 28, bone marrow biopsy with aspirate showed a hypercellular marrow (98% cellularity) with 76% myeloblasts with maturation. Blasts were positive for CD34, CD117, CD33, CD13, HLA-DR, MPO, and showed partial expression of CD15 and CD65 indicating granulocytic maturation. Auer rods were prominent.
At age 28, next-generation sequencing identified FLT3-ITD mutation with allelic ratio 0.44, NPM1 mutation (type A insertion), and WT1 mutation. No DNMT3A, TP53, or ASXL1 mutations were detected.

At age 28, cytogenetic analysis demonstrated a normal female karyotype, 46,XX, in all 20 metaphases. ELN 2022 risk stratification was intermediate based on co-occurrence of FLT3-ITD and NPM1 mutations with normal cytogenetics.

At age 28, induction chemotherapy was initiated with 7+3 cytarabine 200 mg/m2 continuous infusion for seven days and daunorubicin 60 mg/m2 on days one through three, with midostaurin 50 mg orally twice daily on days eight through twenty-one.

At age 28, the patient developed neutropenic fever on day eleven with temperature 39.8 degrees Celsius. CT chest showed tree-in-bud nodularity and ground-glass opacities. Bronchoalveolar lavage grew Aspergillus fumigatus and voriconazole was started with midostaurin dose held temporarily due to drug interaction.
At age 28, grade 3 mucositis developed during the aplastic phase requiring IV narcotics and TPN for nutritional support lasting twelve days.
At age 28, day 14 bone marrow biopsy was markedly hypocellular with less than 5% cellularity and less than 1% blasts, indicating an excellent early clearance of disease.

At age 28, day 28 bone marrow biopsy confirmed complete remission with 1.0% blasts by morphology. Multiparameter flow cytometry MRD was negative at sensitivity of 0.01%. NPM1 mutation transcript was reduced by greater than 4 log.

At age 28, consolidation consisted of four cycles of high-dose cytarabine 3 g/m2 every twelve hours on days one, three, and five, with midostaurin 50 mg twice daily on days eight through twenty-one per cycle.
At age 28, during consolidation cycle two the patient developed febrile neutropenia with Klebsiella pneumoniae bacteremia requiring a 14-day course of meropenem.

At age 28, given intermediate risk with favorable NPM1 co-mutation and deep molecular remission, the tumor board recommended consolidation chemotherapy with midostaurin followed by maintenance midostaurin rather than transplant in first remission.
At age 28, midostaurin maintenance 50 mg orally twice daily was initiated after consolidation, planned for twelve cycles.
At age 28, midostaurin maintenance was complicated by persistent grade 2 nausea and grade 1 diarrhea requiring dose timing adjustment to be taken with food and addition of prophylactic ondansetron.
At age 29, serial MRD monitoring by NPM1 RT-qPCR and flow cytometry at three-month intervals remained negative through six months of maintenance therapy.
At age 29, CT chest abdomen and pelvis at one year follow-up showed no extramedullary disease and normal-appearing spleen and liver.
At age 29, the patient completed twelve cycles of midostaurin maintenance and continued surveillance with quarterly bone marrow biopsies showing ongoing complete molecular remission.

At age 30, at two years from initial diagnosis the patient remained in sustained complete molecular remission with negative FLT3-ITD by PCR and undetectable NPM1 mutation transcript. CBC showed full hematologic recovery with WBC 5,400/uL, hemoglobin 13.8 g/dL, and platelets 198,000/uL.

scenario_blurb: Acute myeloid leukemia (AML) with FLT3-ITD mutation. ELN intermediate/adverse risk. Induction with 7+3 plus midostaurin. Some achieving CR, some refractory. Include cytogenetics (normal karyotype or complex), blast percentage monitoring, allogeneic stem cell transplant in first remission for eligible patients.
